author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm XLII

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We have undertaken the exposition of a Psalm corresponding to your own “longings,” on which we propose to speak to you.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the Psalm itself begins with a certain pious “longing;” and he who sings so, says, “Like as the hart desireth the water-brooks, so longeth my soul after Thee, O God” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Who is it then that saith this?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is ourselves, if we be but willing!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And why ask, who it is other than thyself, when it is in thy power to be the thing which thou art asking about?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is not however one individual, but it is “One Body;” but “Christ’s Body is the Church.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Such “longing” indeed is not found in all who enter the Church: let all however who have “tasted” the sweetness “of the Lord,” and who own in Christ that for which they have a relish, think that they are not the only ones; but that there are such seeds scattered throughout “the field” of the Lord, this whole earth: and that there is a certain Christian unity, whose voice thus speaks, “Like as the hart desireth the water-brooks, so longeth my soul after Thee, O God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And indeed it is not ill understood as the cry of those, who being as yet Catechumens, are hastening to the grace of the holy Font.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: On which account too this Psalm is ordinarily chanted on those occasions, that they may long for the Fountain of remission of sins, even “as the hart for the water-brooks.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let this be allowed; and this meaning retain its place in the Church; a place both truthful and sanctioned by usage.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nevertheless, it appears to me, my brethren, that such “a longing” is not fully satisfied even in the faithful in Baptism: but that haply, if they know where they are sojourning, and whither they have to remove from hence, their “longing” is kindled in even greater intensity.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The title then of it is, “On the end: a Psalm for understanding for the sons of Korah.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We have met with the sons of Korah in other titles of Psalms: and remember to have discussed and stated already the meaning of this name.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Yet we must even now take notice of this title in such a way, that what we have said already should be no prejudice against our saying it again: for all were not present in every place where we said it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now Korah may have been, as indeed he was, a certain definite person; and have had sons, who might be called “the sons of Korah;” let us however search for the secret of which this is the sacrament, that this name may bring to light the mystery with which it is pregnant.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there is some great mystery in the matter that the name “sons of Korah” is given to Christians.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why “sons of Korah”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They are “sons of the bridegroom, sons of Christ.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why then does “Korah” stand for Christ?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because “Korah” is equivalent to “Calvaria.”…Therefore, the “sons of the bridegroom,” the sons of His Passion, the sons redeemed by His Blood, the sons of His Cross, who bear on their forehead that which His enemies erected on Calvary, are called “the sons of Korah;” to them is this Psalm sung as a Psalm for “understanding.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let then our understanding be roused: and if the Psalm be sung to us, let us follow it with our “understanding.”…Run to the brooks; long after the water-brooks.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “With God is the fountain of Life;” a “fountain” that shall never be dried up: in His “Light” is a Light that shall never be darkened.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Long thou for this light: for a certain fountain, a certain light, such as thy bodily eyes know not; a light to see which the inward eye must be prepared; a fountain, to drink of which the inward thirst is to be kindled.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Run to the fountain; long for the fountain; but do it not anyhow, be not satisfied with running like any ordinary animal; run thou “like the hart.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is meant by “like the hart”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let there be no sloth in thy running; run with all thy might: long for the fountain with all thy might.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For we find in “the hart” an emblem of swiftness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But perhaps Scripture meant us to consider in the stag not this point only, but another also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hear what else there is in the hart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It destroys serpents, and after the killing of serpents, it is inflamed with thirst yet more violent; having destroyed serpents, it runs to “the water-brooks,” with thirst more keen than before.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The serpents are thy vices, destroy the serpents of iniquity; then wilt thou long yet more for “the Fountain of Truth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Perhaps avarice whispers in thine ear some dark counsel, hisses against the word of God, hisses against the commandment of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And since it is said to thee, “Disregard this or that thing,” if thou prefer working iniquity to despising some temporal good, thou choosest to be bitten by a serpent, rather than destroy it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whilst, therefore, thou art yet indulgent to thy vice, thy covetousness or thy appetite, when am I to find in thee “a longing” such as this, that might make thee run to the water-brooks?…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is another point to be observed in the hart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is reported of stags…that when they either wander in the herds, or when they are swimming to reach some other parts of the earth, that they support the burdens of their heads on each other, in such a manner as that one takes the lead, and others follow, resting their heads upon him, as again others who follow do upon them, and others in succession to the very end of the herd; but the one who took the lead in bearing the burden of their heads, when tired, returns to the rear, and rests himself after his fatigue by supporting his head just as did the others; by thus supporting what is burdensome, each in turn, they both accomplish their journey, and do not abandon each other.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Are they not a kind of “harts” that the Apostle addresses, saying, “Bear ye one another’s burdens, and so fulfil the Law of Christ”?…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My soul is athirst for the living God” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What I am saying, that “as the hart panteth after the water-brooks, so longs my soul after Thee, O God,” means this, “My soul is athirst for the living God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what is it athirst?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “When shall I come and appear before God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This it is for which I am athirst, to “come and to appear before Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I am athirst in my pilgrimage, in my running; I shall be filled on my arrival.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But “When shall I come?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this, which is soon in the sight of God, is late to our “longing.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “When shall I come and appear before God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This too proceeds from that “longing,” of which in another place comes that cry, “One thing have I desired of the Lord; that will I seek after; that I may dwell in the house of the Lord all the days of my life.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore so?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “That I may behold” (he saith) “the beauty of the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “When shall I come and appear before the Lord?”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My tears have been my meat day and night, while they daily say unto me, Where is thy God?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: My tears (he saith) have been not bitterness, but “my bread.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Those very tears were sweet unto me: being athirst for that fountain, inasmuch as I was not as yet able to drink of it, I have eagerly made my tears my meat.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he said not, “My tears became my drink,” lest he should seem to have longed for them, as for “the water-brooks:” but, still retaining that thirst wherewith I burn, and by which I am hurried away towards the water-brooks, “My tears became my meat,” whilst I am not yet there.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And assuredly he does but the more thirst for the water-brooks from making his tears his meat.…“And they daily say unto me, Where is thy God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if a Pagan should say this to me, I cannot retort it upon him, saying, “Where is thine?” inasmuch as he points with his finger to some stone, and says, “Lo, there is my God!”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When I have laughed at the stone, and he who pointed to it has been put to the blush, he raises his eyes from the stone, looks up to heaven, and perhaps says, pointing his finger to the Sun, “Behold there my God!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Where, I pray, is your God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He has found something to point out to the eyes of the flesh; whereas I, on my part, not that I have not a God to show to him, cannot show him what he has no eyes to see.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he indeed could point out to my bodily eyes his God, the Sun; but what eyes hath he to which I might point out the Creator of the Sun?…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I thought on these things, and poured out my soul above myself” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When would my soul attain to that object of its search, which is “above my soul,” if my soul were not to “pour itself out above itself”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For were it to rest in itself, it would not see anything else beyond itself; and in seeing itself, would not, for all that, see God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let then my insulting enemies now say, “Where is thy God?” aye, let them say it!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I, so long as I do not “see,” so long as my happiness is postponed, make my tears my “bread day and night.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let them still say, “Where is thy God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I seek my God in every corporeal nature, terrestrial or celestial, and find Him not: I seek His Substance in my own soul, and I find it not, yet still I have thought on these things, and wishing to “see the invisible things of my God, being understood by the things made,” I have poured forth my soul above myself, and there remains no longer any being for me to attain to, save my God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is “there” is the “house of my God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: His dwelling-place is above my soul; from thence He beholds me; from thence He created me; from thence He directs me and provides for me; from thence he appeals to me, and calls me, and directs me; leads me in the way, and to the end of my way.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For when I was “pouring out my soul above myself,” in order to reach my God, why did I do so?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For I will go into the place of Thy Tabernacle.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For I should be in error were I to seek for my God without “the place of His tabernacle.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For I will go into the place of Thy wonderful tabernacle, even unto the house of God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I will go,” he says, “into the place of the wonderful tabernacle, even unto the house of God!”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there are already many things that I admire in “the tabernacle.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: See how great wonders I admire in the tabernacle!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For God’s tabernacle on earth is the faithful; I admire in them the obedience of even their bodily members: that in them “Sin does not reign so that they should obey its lusts; neither do they yield their members instruments of unrighteousness unto sin; but unto the living God in good works.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I admire the sight of the bodily members warring in the service of the soul that serves God.…And wonderful though the tabernacle be, yet when I come to “the house of God,” I am even struck dumb with astonishment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Of that “house” he speaks in another Psalm, after he had put a certain abstruse and difficult question to himself (viz., why is it that it generally goes well with the wicked on earth, and ill with the good?), saying, “I thought to know this; it is too painful for me, until I go into the sanctuary of God, and understand of the last things.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is there, in the sanctuary of God, in the house of God, is the fountain of “understanding.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There he “understood of the last things;” and solved the question concerning the prosperity of the unrighteous, and the sufferings of the righteous.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How does he solve it?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why, that the wicked, when reprieved here, are reserved for punishments without end; and the good when they suffer here, are being tried in order that they may in the end obtain the inheritance.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And it was in the sanctuary of God that he understood this, and “understood of the last things.”…For he tells us of his progress, and of his guidance thither; as if we had been saying, “You are admiring the tabernacle here on earth; how came you to the sanctuary of the house of God?” he says, “In the voice of joy and praise; the sound of keeping holiday.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here, when men keep festival simply for their own indulgence, it is their custom to place musical instruments, or to station a chorus of singers, before their houses, or any kind of music that serves and allures to wantonness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And when these are heard, what do we passers by say?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “What is going on here?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And we are told in answer, that it is some festival.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “It is a birthday that is being celebrated” (say they), “there is a marriage here;” that those songs may not appear out of place, but the luxurious indulgence may be excused by the festive occasion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In the “house of God” there is a never-ending festival: for there it is not an occasion celebrated once, and then to pass away.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The angelic choir makes an eternal “holiday:” the presence of God’s face, joy that never fails.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is a “holiday” of such a kind, as neither to be opened by any dawn, nor terminated by any evening.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From that everlasting perpetual festivity, a certain sweet and melodious strain strikes on the ears of the heart, provided only the world do not drown the sounds.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As he walks in this tabernacle, and contemplates God’s wonderful works for the redemption of the faithful, the sound of that festivity charms his ears, and bears the “hart” away to “the water-brooks.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But seeing, brethren, so long as “we are at home in this body, we are absent from the Lord;” and “the corruptible body presseth down the soul, and the earthly tabernacle weigheth down the mind that museth on many things;” even though we have some way or other dispersed the clouds, by walking as “longing” leads us on, and for a brief while have come within reach of that sound, so that by an effort we may catch something from that “house of God,” yet through the burden, so to speak, of our infirmity, we sink back to our usual level, and relapse to our ordinary state.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And just as there we found cause for rejoicing, so here there will not be wanting an occasion for sorrow.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that hart that made “tears” its “bread day and night,” borne along by “longing to the water-brooks” (that is, to the spiritual delights of God), “pouring forth his soul above himself,” that he may attain to what is “above” his own soul, walking towards “the place of the wonderful tabernacle, even unto the house of God,” and led on by the sweetness of that inward spiritual sound to feel contempt for all outward things, and be borne on to things spiritual, is but a mortal man still; is still groaning here, still bearing about the frailty of flesh, still in peril in the midst of the “offences” of this world.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He therefore glances back to himself, as if he were coming from that world; and says to himself, now placed in the midst of these sorrows, comparing these with the things, to see which he had entered in there, and after seeing which he had come forth from thence;

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Why art thou cast down, O my soul, and why dost thou disquiet me?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lo, we have just now been gladdened by certain inward delights: with the mind’s eye we have been able to behold, though but with a momentary glance, something not susceptible of change: why dost thou still “disquiet me, why art thou” still “cast down”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For thou dost not doubt of thy God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For now thou art not without somewhat to say to thyself, in answer to those who say, “Where is thy God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I have now had the perception of something that is unchangeable; why dost thou disquiet me still?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Hope in God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Just as if his soul was silently replying to him, “Why do I disquiet thee, but because I am not yet there, where that delight is, to which I was, as it were, rapt for a moment?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Am I already ‘drinking’ from this ‘fountain’ with nothing to fear?”…Still “Hope in God,” is his answer to the soul that disquiets him, and would fain account for her disquiet from the evils with which this world abounds.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In the mean while dwell in hope: for “hope that is seen is not hope; but if we hope for that we see not, then do we with patience wait for it.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Hope in God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For I will confess unto Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What wilt thou “confess”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My God is the saving health of my countenance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: My “health” (my salvation) cannot be from myself; this it is that I will say, that I will “confess.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is my God that is “the saving health of my countenance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For to account for his fears, in the midst of those things, which he now knows, having come after a sort to the “understanding” of them, he has been looking behind him again in anxiety, lest the enemy be stealing upon him: he cannot yet say, “I am made whole every whit.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For having but “the first-fruits of the Spirit, we groan within ourselves; waiting for the adoption, to wit, the redemption of the body.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When that “health” (that salvation) is perfected in us, then shall we be living in the house of God for ever, and praising for ever Him to whom it was said, “Blessed are they that dwell in Thy house, they will be praising Thee world without end.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is not so yet, because the salvation which is promised, is not as yet in being; but it is “in hope” that I confess unto God, and say, “My God is the saving health of my countenance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is “in hope” that “we are saved; but hope that is seen, is not hope.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My soul is disquieted on account of myself” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it disquieted on account of God?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is on my own account it is disquieted.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By the Unchangeable it was revived; it is by the changeable it is disquieted.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I know that the righteousness of God remaineth; whether my own will remain stedfast, I know not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For I am alarmed by the Apostle’s saying, “Let him that thinketh he standeth, take heed lest he fall.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore since “there is no soundness in me for myself,” there is no hope either for me of myself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My soul is disquieted on account of myself.”…“Therefore I remember Thee, O Lord, from the land of Jordan, and from the little hill of Hermon.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From whence did I remember thee?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From the “little hill,” and from the “land of Jordan.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Perhaps from Baptism, where the remission of sins is given.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For no one runs to the remission of sins, except he who is dissatisfied with himself; no one runs to the remission of sins, but he who confesses himself a sinner; no one confesses himself a sinner, except by humbling himself before God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore it is from “the land of Jordan I have remembered thee, and from the hill;” observe, not “of the great hill,” that thou mayest make of the “little hill” a great one: for “whoso exalteth himself shall be abased, and whoso humbleth himself shall be exalted.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If you would also ask the meanings of the names, Jordan means “their descent.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Descend then, that thou mayest be “lifted up:” be not lifted up, lest thou be cast down.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And the little hill of Hermon.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hermon means “anathematizing.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Anathematize thyself, by being displeased with thyself; for if thou art pleased with thyself, God will be displeased with thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because then God gives us all good things, because He Himself is good, not because we are worthy of it; because He is merciful, not because we have in anything deserved it; it is from “the land of Jordan, and from Hermon,” that I remember thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And because he so remembers with humility, he shall earn his exaltation to fruition, for he is not “exalted” in himself, who “glories in the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deep calleth unto deep with the voice of thy water-spouts” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I may perhaps finish the Psalm, aided as I am by your attention, whose fervour I perceive.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As for your fatigue in hearing, I am not greatly solicitous, since you see me also, who speak, toiling in the heat of these exertions.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Assuredly it is from your seeing me labouring, that you labour with me: for I am labouring not for myself, but for you.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deep calleth unto deep with the voice of thy water-spouts.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It was God whom he addressed, who “remembered him from the land of Jordan and Hermon.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It was in wonder and admiration he spake this: “Abyss calleth unto abyss with the voice of Thy water-spouts.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What abyss is this that calls, and to what other abyss?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Justly, because the “understanding” spoken of is an “abyss.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For an “abyss” is a depth that cannot be reached or comprehended; and it is principally applied to a great body of water.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there is a “depth,” a “profound,” the bottom of which cannot be reached by sounding.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Furthermore, it is said in a certain passage, “Thy judgments are a mighty abyss,” Scripture meaning to suggest that the judgments of God are incomprehensible.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What then is the “abyss” that calls, and to what other “abyss” does it call?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If by “abyss” we understand a great depth, is not man’s heart, do you not suppose, “an abyss”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what is there more profound than that “abyss”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Men may speak, may be seen by the operations of their members, may be heard speaking in conversation: but whose thought is penetrated, whose heart seen into?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What he is inwardly engaged on, what he is inwardly capable of, what he is inwardly doing or what purposing, what he is inwardly wishing to happen, or not to happen, who shall comprehend?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I think an “abyss” may not unreasonably be understood of man, of whom it is said elsewhere, “Man shall come to a deep heart, and God shall be exalted.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If man then is an “abyss,” in what way doth “abyss” call on “abyss”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Does man “call on” man as God is called upon?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: No, but “calls on” is equivalent to “calls to him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it was said of a certain person, he calls on death; that is, lives in such a way as to be inviting death; for there is no man at all who puts up a prayer, and calls expressly on death: but men by evil-living invite death.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deep calls on deep,” then, is, “man calls to man.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus is it wisdom is learnt, and thus faith, when “man calls to man.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The holy preachers of God’s word call on the “deep:” are they not themselves “a deep” also?…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deep calleth to deep with the voice of Thy water-spouts.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I, who tremble all over, when my soul was disquieted on account of myself, feared greatly on account of Thy “judgments.”…Are those judgments slight ones?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They are great ones, severe, hard to bear; but would they were all.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deep calls to deep with the voice of Thy water-spouts,” in that Thou threatenest, Thou sayest, that there is another condemnation in store even after those sufferings.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deep calls on deep with the voice of Thy water-spouts.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Whither then shall I go from Thy presence?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And whither shall I flee from Thy Spirit?” seeing that deep calls to deep, and after those sufferings severer ones are to be dreaded.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “All Thy overhangings and Thy waves are come upon me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The “waves” in what I already feel, the “overhangings” in that Thou denouncest.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All my sufferings are Thy waves; all Thy denouncements of judgments are Thy “overhangings.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In the “waves” that deep “calleth;” in the “overhangings” is the other “deep” which it “calls to.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In this that I suffer are all Thy waves; in the severer punishment that Thou threatenest, all Thy “overhangings” are come unto me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He who threatens does not let His judgments fall upon us, but keeps them suspended over us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But inasmuch as Thou sittest at liberty, I have thus spoken unto my soul.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Hope in God: for I will confess unto Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: My God is the saving health of my countenance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The more numerous my sufferings, the sweeter will be Thy mercy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore follows: “The Lord will commend His loving-kindness in the day-time; and in the night-time will He declare it” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In tribulation no man has leisure to hear: attend, when it is well with you; hear, when it is well with you; learn, when you are in tranquillity, the discipline of wisdom, and store up the word of God as you do food.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in tribulation every one must be profited by what he heard in the time of security.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in prosperity God “commends to thee His mercy,” in case thou serve Him faithfully, for He frees thee from tribulation; but it is “in the night” only that He “declares” His mercy to thee, which He “commended” to thee by day.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When tribulation shall actually come, He will not leave thee destitute of His help; He will show thee that which He commended to thee in the daytime is true.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is written in a certain passage, “The mercy of the Lord is seasonable in the time of affliction, as clouds of rain in the time of drought.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The Lord hath commended His loving-kindness in the day-time, and in the night will He declare it.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He does not show that He is thine Helper, unless tribulation come, from whence thou must be rescued by Him who promised it to thee “in the day-time.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore we are warned to be like “the ant.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For just as worldly prosperity is signified by “the day,” adversity by the night, so again in another way worldly prosperity is expressed by “the summer,” adversity by the winter.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And what is it that the ant does?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: She lays up in summer what will be useful to her in winter.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whilst therefore it is summer, whilst it is well with you, whilst you are in tranquillity, hear the word of the Lord.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For how can it be that in the midst of these tempests of the world, you should pass through the whole of that sea, without suffering?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How could it happen?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To what mortal’s lot has it fallen?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If even it has been the lot of any, that very calm is more to be dreaded.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The Lord hath commended His loving-kindness in the day-time, and in the night-time will He declare it.”…“There is with me prayer unto the God of my life.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This I make my business here; I who am the “hart thirsting and longing for the water-brooks,” calling to mind the sweetness of that strain, by which I was led on through the tabernacle even to the house of God; whilst this “corruptible body presseth down the soul,” there is yet with me “prayer unto the God of my life.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in order to making supplication unto God, I have not to buy aught from places beyond the sea; or in order that He may hear me, have I to sail to bring from a distance frankincense and perfumes, or have I to bring “calf or ram from the flock.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is “with me prayer to the God of my life.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I have within a victim to sacrifice; I have within an incense to place on the altar; I have within a sacrifice wherewith to propitiate my God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The sacrifice of God is a troubled spirit.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What sacrifice of a “troubled spirit” I have within, hear.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I will say unto God, Thou art my lifter up.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why hast Thou forgotten me?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For I am suffering here, even as if Thou hadst forgotten me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But Thou art trying me, and I know that Thou dost but put off, not take utterly from me, what Thou hast promised me.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But yet, “Why hast Thou forgotten me?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So cried our Head also, as if speaking in our name.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “My God, my God, why hast Thou forsaken me?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I will say unto God, “Thou art my lifter up; why hast Thou forgotten me?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Why hast Thou rejected me?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Rejected” me, that is to say, from that height of the apprehension of the unchangeable Truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Why hast Thou rejected me?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why, when already longing for those things, have I been cast down to these, by the weight and burden of my iniquity?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This same voice in another passage said, “I said in my trance” (i.e., in my rapture, when he had seen some great thing or other), “I said in my trance, I am cast out of the sight of Thine eyes.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he compared these things in which he found himself, to those toward which he had been raised; and saw himself cast out far “from the sight of God’s eyes,” as he speaks even here, “Why hast Thou rejected me?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why go I mourning, while mine enemy troubleth me, while he breaketh my bones?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Even he, my tempter, the devil; while offences are everywhere on the increase, because of the abundance of which “the love of many is waxing cold.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When we see the strong members of the Church generally giving way to the causes of offence, does not Christ’s body say, “The enemy breaketh my bones”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is the strong members that are “the bones;” and sometimes even those that are strong sink under their temptations.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For whosoever of the body of Christ considers this, does he not exclaim, with the voice of Christ’s Body, “Why hast Thou rejected me?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why go I mourning, while mine enemy troubleth me, while he breaketh my bones?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You may see not my flesh merely, but even my “bones.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To see those who were thought to have some stability, giving way under temptations, so that the rest of the weak brethren despair when they see those who are strong succumbing; how great, my brethren, are the dangers!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They who trouble me cast me in the teeth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Again that voice!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “While they say daily unto me, Where is thy God?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And it is principally in the temptations of the Church they say this, “Where is thy God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How much was this cast in the teeth of the Martyrs!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Those men so patient and courageous for the name of Christ, how often was it said to them, “Where is your God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let Him deliver you, if He can.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For men saw their torments outwardly; they did not inwardly behold their crowns!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They who trouble me cast me in the teeth, while they say daily unto me, Where is thy God?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And on this account, seeing “my soul is disquieted on account of myself,” what else should I say unto it than those words:

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Why art thou cast down, O my soul; and why dost thou disquiet me?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And, as it seems to answer, “Wouldest thou not have me disquiet thee, placed as I am here in so great evils?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wouldest thou have me not disquiet thee, panting as I am after what is good, thirsting and labouring as I am for it?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What should I say, but,

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Hope thou in God; for I will yet confess unto Him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He states the very words of that confession; he repeats the grounds on which he fortifies his hope.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He is the health of my countenance, and my God.”
